docs.title: @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance
docs.text:
60 opinionated ESLint rules for TypeScript teams that refuse to compromise on code quality.

Supports ESLint 8.57+, 9.x, and 10.x

docs.title: Why Zero Tolerance?
docs.text:
Most linting setups start strict and erode over time. A scattered eslint-disable here, an any cast there, and before long the rules exist in name only.

This plugin takes the opposite approach. Every rule earns its place, every violation is actionable, and the codebase stays honest.

docs.title: What It Covers
docs.text:
@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance provides rules across eight categories:

Category What it enforces Type Safety Ban unsafe assertions (as, !), literal unions, inline imports, and untyped exports Code Quality Limit function size and parameters, eliminate magic values, enforce immutability and sort order Testing Require should-prefixed descriptions, ban leaky mocks, imprecise matchers, and timer/fetch abuse Bug Prevention Flag identical expressions, redundant booleans, missing awaits, floating promises, and flag arguments Error Handling Require new Error() throws, ban empty catches, encourage Result-style returns Imports Keep barrels clean, ban dynamic imports, prevent re-exports and parent-directory traversal Documentation Require JSDoc on functions, enforce BDD specs, mandate optional chaining and readonly props Naming Enforce I-prefixed interfaces

docs.title: Packages
docs.text:
This monorepo publishes two packages:

Package Description @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance The ESLint plugin with all custom rules @coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance Pre-built recommended and strict config presets

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
npm install --save-dev @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance

// eslint.config.js (ESLint 9+ Flat Config)
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.recommended];

See the Getting Started guide for full setup instructions and the Rules reference for descriptions of every rule.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance ships with four built-in config presets so you can go from zero to linting in one line.

docs.title: Preset Overview
docs.text: Preset Severity Use case recommended warn Gradual adoption; shows violations without breaking the build strict error New projects or full zero-tolerance enforcement; fails on any violation legacy-recommended warn ESLint 8 .eslintrc format legacy-strict error ESLint 8 .eslintrc format

docs.title: Preset Enum
docs.text:
Internally, flat-config preset selection is typed with a Preset enum:

  • Preset.Recommended = 'recommended'
  • Preset.Strict = 'strict'

This enum is used by the shared config/rule builders to avoid stringly-typed preset branching.

docs.title: recommended
docs.text:
Most rules enabled at warn (with a few documented exceptions disabled). Function body limit: 30 lines. Parameter limit: 4.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.recommended];

docs.title: strict
docs.text:
All rules enabled at error. Function body limit: 25 lines. Parameter limit: 4.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.strict];

docs.title: legacy-recommended
legacy-strict
Rules Included in Each Preset
docs.text:
Nearly all core rules are included in every preset. require-bdd-spec, no-parent-internal-access, no-fetch-in-tests, and no-restricted-imports-in-tests are available as opt-in rules and are disabled by default in the built-in presets. Other preset differences are the severity level and the tighter limits for configurable rules.

Rule Recommended Strict max-function-lines warn (max: 30) error (max: 25) max-params warn (max: 4) error (max: 4) no-array-mutation warn error no-await-in-loop warn error no-banned-types warn error no-barrel-parent-imports warn error no-boolean-return-trap warn error no-date-now warn error no-destructured-parameter-type-literal warn error no-dynamic-import warn error no-empty-catch warn error no-eslint-disable warn error no-export-alias warn error no-fetch-in-tests off off no-flag-argument warn error no-floating-promises warn error no-for-in warn error no-identical-branches warn error no-identical-expressions warn error no-inline-type-import warn error no-jest-have-been-called warn error no-labels warn error no-literal-property-unions warn error no-literal-unions warn error no-magic-numbers warn error no-magic-strings warn error no-mock-implementation warn error no-non-null-assertion warn error no-object-mutation warn error no-parameter-reassign warn error no-parent-internal-access off off no-query-side-effects warn error no-re-export warn error no-redundant-boolean warn error no-restricted-imports-in-tests off off no-set-interval-in-tests warn error no-set-timeout-in-tests warn error no-test-interface-declaration warn error no-throw-literal warn error no-type-assertion warn error no-with warn error prefer-guard-clauses warn error prefer-nullish-coalescing warn error prefer-readonly-parameters warn error prefer-result-return off warn prefer-shortcut-return warn error prefer-string-raw warn error require-bdd-spec off off require-clean-barrel warn error require-exported-object-type warn error require-interface-prefix warn error require-jsdoc-anonymous-functions off warn require-jsdoc-functions warn error require-node-protocol warn error require-optional-chaining warn error require-readonly-props warn error require-test-description-style warn error require-union-type-alias warn error sort-functions warn error sort-imports warn error

require-bdd-spec, no-parent-internal-access, no-fetch-in-tests, and no-restricted-imports-in-tests are available but intentionally not enabled by default in presets.

docs.title: Disabling Individual Rules
docs.text:
Override any rule to off inside your config:

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [
  zeroTolerance.configs.recommended,
  {
    rules: {
      // Disable rules that don't suit your project
      'zero-tolerance/sort-functions': 'off',
    },
  },
];

docs.title: Configuring Rule Options
docs.text: Multiple rules accept an options object:

docs.title: max-function-lines
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['warn', { max: 50 }]

docs.title: max-params
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/max-params': ['error', { max: 3 }]

docs.title: no-magic-strings
docs.text:
'zero-tolerance/no-magic-strings': [
  'error',
  { checkComparisons: true, checkSwitchCases: true, ignoreValues: ['production'] },
]

docs.title: require-test-description-style
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/require-test-description-style': ['error', { prefix: 'should', ignoreSkip: true }]

docs.title: no-restricted-imports-in-tests
docs.text:
'zero-tolerance/no-restricted-imports-in-tests': [
  'error',
  { modules: ['fs', 'node:fs/*', 'child_process', 'axios', '@company/database'] },
]

Configured module names are trimmed, lowercased, normalized by removing node:, and matched by module root. For example, fs matches fs, fs/promises, node:fs, and node:fs/promises.

docs.title: no-throw-literal
docs.text:
'zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal': [
  'error',
  {
    allowThrowingCallExpressions: false,
    allowThrowingMemberExpressions: false,
    allowThrowingAwaitExpressions: false,
  },
]

docs.title: no-parent-internal-access
docs.text:
'zero-tolerance/no-parent-internal-access': [
  'error',
  { protectedDirectories: ['src', 'app', 'internal'] },
]

The rule matches only the first concrete directory reached after .. traversal, so ../src/foo is reported but ../shared/src/foo is not unless shared is also protected. Configured directory names are trimmed and deduplicated, and empty entries are ignored.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Get from zero to linting in under five minutes. This guide covers installation, preset selection, and common project setups.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Dependency Version Node.js 18+ (for ESLint 8.57+ or 9.x), 20+ (recommended for 10.x) TypeScript 5.x ESLint 8.57+ or 9.x (Node 18+), or 10.x (Node 20+ recommended) @typescript-eslint/parser 8.x

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
npmpnpmyarn 
npm install --save-dev @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance @typescript-eslint/parser

pnpm add -D @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance @typescript-eslint/parser

yarn add -D @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance @typescript-eslint/parser

docs.title: Recommended preset
docs.text:
The recommended preset enables all rules at warn severity, making it easy to adopt incrementally.

Start here

The recommended preset is the best starting point for existing projects. Warnings surface issues without breaking your build, giving your team time to address violations at a sustainable pace.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [
  zeroTolerance.configs.recommended,
  // your other configs...
];

docs.title: Strict preset
docs.text:
The strict preset raises most rules to error severity and applies tighter limits (e.g. max function body: 25 lines); see the preset table for exact rule severities.

Best for greenfield projects

The strict preset treats every violation as a build-breaking error. It is ideal for new projects or teams ready for full enforcement.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.strict];

docs.title: Custom configuration
docs.text:
Pick individual rules and set severities yourself:

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [
  {
    plugins: {
      'zero-tolerance': zeroTolerance,
    },
    rules: {
      'zero-tolerance/require-interface-prefix': 'error',
      'zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal': 'error',
      'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['warn', { max: 40 }],
      'zero-tolerance/max-params': ['warn', { max: 5 }],
      // ... add any rules you need
    },
  },
];

docs.title: Using the separate config package
docs.text:
If you prefer to import pre-built config presets as separate modules:

npm install --save-dev @coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance
eslint.config.js
import recommended from '@coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance/recommended';
// or
import strict from '@coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance/strict';

export default [
  recommended, // or strict
];

docs.title: ESLint 8.x (Legacy .eslintrc)
Typical TypeScript Project Setup
docs.text:
A complete eslint.config.js for a TypeScript project:

eslint.config.js
import js from '@eslint/js';
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint';
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default tseslint.config(
  js.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  zeroTolerance.configs.recommended,
  {
    files: ['src/**/*.ts'],
    rules: {
      // Override individual rules as needed
      'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['warn', { max: 50 }],
    },
  },
  {
    // Relax rules in test files
    files: ['**/*.test.ts', '**/*.spec.ts'],
    rules: {
      'zero-tolerance/require-jsdoc-functions': 'off',
      'zero-tolerance/no-type-assertion': 'off',
    },
  },
);

docs.title: Configuring tsconfig.json
docs.text:
No TypeScript-specific configuration is required by this plugin. All rules operate purely on the AST without requiring type information.

No type-aware linting required

Unlike some TypeScript-ESLint rules, zero-tolerance rules do not need parserOptions.project or projectService. This keeps lint times fast regardless of project size.

tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Browse the Rules reference for descriptions, examples, and severity settings for every rule.
  • Read the Configuration page to understand the difference between the recommended and strict presets.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance provides 60 custom rules grouped into eight categories. Each rule links to a dedicated page with rationale, correct/incorrect examples, and configuration options.

Category Rules Focus Naming Conventions 1 Interface naming standards Documentation 5 JSDoc, BDD specs, optional chaining, readonly props Testing 8 Test descriptions, mocks, timers, fetch, interfaces Type Safety 9 Assertions, unions, imports, exported types Code Quality 12 Function size, magic values, immutability, sorting Error Handling 3 Throw safety, empty catches, Result patterns Imports 7 Barrels, re-exports, dynamic imports, node protocol Bug Prevention 15 Identical code, control flow, async safety

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-interface-prefix suggestion Enforce that interface names start with I

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-bdd-spec suggestion Enforce that every TypeScript source file has a valid sibling .ts.bdd.json BDD spec require-jsdoc-anonymous-functions suggestion Require JSDoc comments on anonymous function-like constructs except test files and known test callbacks require-jsdoc-functions suggestion Require JSDoc comments on named functions (except test files) require-optional-chaining suggestion Require optional chaining instead of repeated guard access require-readonly-props suggestion Require JSX component props to be typed as readonly

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-test-description-style suggestion Enforce that test descriptions start with should no-jest-have-been-called suggestion Prohibit imprecise call-assertion matchers; use toHaveBeenCalledTimes and toHaveBeenNthCalledWith instead no-mock-implementation suggestion Prohibit persistent mock methods; use Once variants to prevent test bleeds no-set-timeout-in-tests suggestion Disallow setTimeout usage in test files no-set-interval-in-tests suggestion Disallow setInterval usage in test files no-fetch-in-tests suggestion Disallow fetch usage in test files no-restricted-imports-in-tests suggestion Disallow configured dependency imports in test files no-test-interface-declaration suggestion Disallow interface declarations in test files; import production types instead

docs.title: Type Safety
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-type-assertion suggestion Prevent use of TypeScript as and angle-bracket assertions no-non-null-assertion problem Disallow non-null assertions using the ! postfix operator no-literal-unions suggestion Ban literal union types in favour of enums no-literal-property-unions suggestion Require property literal unions to use named domain types require-union-type-alias suggestion Require inline union types with multiple type references to use named type aliases no-banned-types problem Ban ReturnType and indexed access types no-inline-type-import problem Disallow inline import("...") type annotations no-destructured-parameter-type-literal suggestion Disallow inline object type literals on destructured parameters require-exported-object-type suggestion Require exported object constants to declare an explicit type annotation

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text: Rule Type Description max-function-lines suggestion Enforce a maximum number of lines per function body max-params suggestion Enforce a maximum number of function parameters no-array-mutation suggestion Disallow mutating array methods no-date-now suggestion Disallow Date.now() and no-arg new Date() usage no-magic-numbers suggestion Disallow magic numbers; use named constants instead no-magic-strings suggestion Disallow magic strings in comparisons and switch cases no-object-mutation suggestion Disallow direct object-property mutation sort-imports suggestion Require import declarations to be ordered by group and alphabetically within each group sort-functions suggestion Require top-level functions and const function expressions to be sorted alphabetically prefer-nullish-coalescing suggestion Prefer nullish coalescing instead of repeated nullish guard ternaries prefer-readonly-parameters suggestion Prefer readonly typing for object and array-like parameters prefer-string-raw suggestion Prefer String.raw for strings containing escaped backslashes

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-empty-catch problem Disallow empty catch blocks that silently swallow errors no-throw-literal problem Disallow throwing literals, objects, or templates; always throw a new Error instance prefer-result-return suggestion Prefer returning Result-style values instead of throwing

docs.title: Imports
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-clean-barrel suggestion Require barrel files (index.*) to contain only module re-exports no-dynamic-import problem Ban dynamic import() and require() outside test files no-export-alias suggestion Prevent use of aliases in export statements no-barrel-parent-imports suggestion Disallow parent-directory traversal in barrel-file import paths no-parent-internal-access suggestion Disallow parent-relative access into protected internal directories such as src no-re-export suggestion Disallow direct or pass-through re-exports from parent/grandparent modules require-node-protocol suggestion Require Node.js built-in module imports to use the node: protocol prefix

docs.title: Bug Prevention
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-identical-expressions problem Disallow identical expressions on both sides of a binary or logical operator no-identical-branches suggestion Disallow identical conditional branches no-boolean-return-trap suggestion Disallow ambiguous boolean-return APIs outside predicate naming no-redundant-boolean suggestion Disallow redundant comparisons to boolean literals no-for-in problem Disallow for..in loops no-labels problem Disallow labeled statements no-with problem Disallow with statements no-await-in-loop problem Disallow await inside loops; use Promise.all() instead no-floating-promises problem Disallow unhandled promise expressions; require explicit handling no-eslint-disable suggestion Prevent use of eslint-disable comments no-parameter-reassign suggestion Disallow reassigning function parameters no-flag-argument suggestion Disallow boolean flag parameters in function signatures prefer-guard-clauses suggestion Prefer guard clauses by removing else blocks after terminating if branches prefer-shortcut-return suggestion Prefer shortcut boolean returns over if branches that return true/false no-query-side-effects suggestion Disallow side effects in query-style functions

docs.title: max-function-lines
docs.text: Enforce a maximum number of lines per function body.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn (max: 30) Strict error (max: 25)

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Long functions are harder to understand, test, and maintain. Keeping functions small forces a single-responsibility design: each function does one thing, has a clear name, and can be tested in isolation. This rule counts the lines of a function's block body (inclusive of opening and closing braces) and reports any function that exceeds the configured limit. Arrow functions with concise expression bodies (no block) are not checked.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Validates a user registration payload.
 */
function validateRegistration(payload: IRegistrationPayload): IValidationResult {
  const errors: string[] = [];
  if (!payload.email) errors.push('Email is required');
  if (!payload.password) errors.push('Password is required');
  return { valid: errors.length === 0, errors };
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect (exceeds limit)
docs.text:
function doEverything(user: IUser) {
  // 35+ lines of mixed concerns...
}

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Type Default Description max number 30 Maximum allowed lines in a function body

The standalone default is 30 lines. The recommended preset keeps this at 30 and the strict preset sets it to 25.

// Use the recommended preset value (30 lines)
'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': 'warn'

// Custom limit
'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['error', { max: 50 }]

docs.title: max-params
docs.text: Enforce a maximum number of function parameters.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn (max: 4) Strict error (max: 4)

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Functions with many parameters are hard to call correctly, difficult to read, and a sign that the function is doing too much or that related parameters should be grouped into a structured options object. Limiting the parameter count encourages better API design.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Creates a new user record.
 */
function createUser(options: ICreateUserOptions): Promise<IUser> {
  return db.users.create(options);
}

/**
 * Sends an email notification.
 */
function sendEmail(to: string, subject: string, body: string): Promise<void> {
  return mailer.send({ to, subject, body });
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function createUser(
  name: string,
  email: string,
  password: string,
  role: string,
  createdAt: Date,
): Promise<IUser> {
  // Too many positional parameters
}

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Type Default Description max number 4 Maximum number of allowed parameters 
// Default (max 4)
'zero-tolerance/max-params': 'warn'

// Custom limit
'zero-tolerance/max-params': ['error', { max: 3 }]

docs.title: no-array-mutation
docs.text: Disallow mutating array methods.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Mutating arrays in place creates hidden side effects. Immutable array operations make behavior easier to reason about and safer to refactor.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: const next = [...items, value];

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text: items.push(value);

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/no-array-mutation': 'error'

docs.title: no-await-in-loop
docs.text: Disallow await expressions inside loop bodies; use Promise.all() or Promise.allSettled() instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
await inside a loop serialises asynchronous operations: each iteration waits for the previous one to finish before starting the next. This is almost never the intended behaviour and is a significant performance bottleneck. Promise.all() runs the operations in parallel, completing in the time of the slowest single operation rather than the sum of all operations.

The rule detects await inside for, for...in, for...of, while, and do...while loops. It does not flag await inside callbacks or async functions nested within the loop body.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Run all requests in parallel
const results = await Promise.all(ids.map((id) => fetchUser(id)));

// Settle all, collecting errors
const settled = await Promise.allSettled(items.map((item) => process(item)));

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
for (const id of ids) {
  // Serialised — each request waits for the previous
  const user = await fetchUser(id);
  users.push(user);
}

while (queue.length > 0) {
  const item = queue.shift()!;
  await process(item);
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-await-in-loop': 'error'

docs.title: no-banned-types
docs.text: Ban ReturnType utility type and TypeScript indexed access types (T["key"]).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
ReturnType<typeof fn> couples callers to the internal return type of a function rather than an explicit, named contract. When the function's return type changes, the inferred type silently changes everywhere it is used, bypassing deliberate type design.

Indexed access types (T["key"]) expose internal structure, making refactoring brittle and obscuring intent. Prefer explicit type aliases or interface members instead.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Declare an explicit return type
type FetchResult = { data: IUser; status: number };

async function fetchUser(): Promise<FetchResult> {
  // ...
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// ReturnType couples callers to implementation details
type MyReturnType = ReturnType<typeof fetchUser>;

// Indexed access exposes internal structure
type UserName = IUser['name'];

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-banned-types': 'error'

docs.title: no-barrel-parent-imports
docs.text: Disallow parent-directory traversal inside barrel files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Barrel files (index.*) should aggregate exports from their own directory. Parent-directory imports (.. and ../*) inside those files create upward coupling, blur package boundaries, and make the barrel harder to reason about.

This rule is enabled by default in plugin presets: warn in recommended and error in strict.

This rule applies only when the current file is a barrel file (index.*). In those files, it checks:

  • import ... from '...'
  • import('...')
  • require('...')
  • TypeScript import x = require('...')

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The rule applies only to single-extension barrel files such as index.ts, index.js, and index.mts.
  • Double-extension files such as index.d.ts, index.test.ts, and index.spec.js are ignored.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// feature.ts
import React from 'react';
import { parseUser } from '../parse-user';
const helpers = await import('../helpers');
const feature = require('../feature');
import legacyFeature = require('../legacy-feature');

// index.ts
export { parseUser } from './parse-user';
export { helpers } from './helpers';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// index.ts
import { parseUser } from '../parse-user';
import '..';

const helpers = await import('../helpers');
const feature = require('../../feature');
import legacyFeature = require('..');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-barrel-parent-imports': 'error'

docs.title: no-boolean-return-trap
docs.text: Disallow ambiguous boolean-return APIs outside predicate naming.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Boolean returns can be ambiguous at call sites. Public APIs should either use predicate naming (is*, has*, can*, should*) or return richer result types.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function isReady(user: User): boolean {
  return user.status === 'ready';
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function validateUser(user: User): boolean {
  return user.status === 'ready';
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/no-boolean-return-trap': 'error'

docs.title: no-date-now
docs.text: Disallow Date.now() and new Date() without arguments.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Direct time reads create non-deterministic code that is harder to test. Injecting a clock or passing timestamps improves testability and reproducibility.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: const timestamp = clock.now();

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const timestamp = Date.now();
const createdAt = new Date();

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/no-date-now': 'error'

docs.title: no-destructured-parameter-type-literal
docs.text: Disallow inline object type literals on destructured parameters.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Destructured parameters already hide the original object shape at the call site. Adding an inline object type literal to the same parameter makes the contract harder to reuse, harder to scan, and harder to name in documentation. Requiring a named type keeps the contract explicit and reusable.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
interface IUiActions {
  readonly set: UiStateSetter;
}

function update({ set }: IUiActions): void {
  set('ready');
}

type UiActions = Readonly<{ readonly set: UiStateSetter }>;

const updateLater = ({ set }: UiActions): void => {
  set('later');
};

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function update({ set }: Readonly<{ set: UiStateSetter }>): void {
  set('ready');
}

const updateLater = ({ set }: { readonly set: UiStateSetter }): void => {
  set('later');
};

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-destructured-parameter-type-literal': 'error'

docs.title: no-dynamic-import
docs.text: Ban dynamic import() and require() calls outside of test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Dynamic imports and require() calls load modules at runtime rather than at bundle time. This hides dependencies from static analysis tools, prevents tree-shaking, complicates code-splitting strategies, and can introduce subtle load-order bugs. Static import declarations at the top of a file are always preferable in application code.

The rule is automatically skipped in test files (*.test.*, *.spec.*, *.e2e.*, *.integration.*, and files under __tests__/), where dynamic loading is sometimes required by test utilities.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Static import at the top of the file
import { parseConfig } from './config-parser';

// Allowed in test files: mymodule.test.ts
const module = await import('./module');
import('./module').then((m) => m.default);
const pkg = require('./package');

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect (in non-test files)
docs.text:
// In mymodule.ts
const module = await import('./heavy-module');
import('./heavy-module').then((m) => m.default);

const pkg = require('some-package');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-dynamic-import': 'error'

docs.title: no-empty-catch
docs.text: Disallow empty catch blocks that silently swallow errors.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: An empty catch block discards the error entirely. The caller has no indication that anything went wrong, diagnostics are impossible, and bugs become untraceable. Every caught error must be handled in some meaningful way: logged, re-thrown, returned as a result, or surfaced to the user.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
try {
  riskyOperation();
} catch (err) {
  logger.error('riskyOperation failed', err);
}

try {
  connectToDb();
} catch (err) {
  throw new Error(`Database connection failed: ${err}`);
}

try {
  cleanup();
} catch {
  throw new Error('Cleanup step failed');
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
try {
  riskyOperation();
} catch (err) {}

try {
  connectToDb();
} catch {}

try {
  cleanup();
} catch (err) {
} finally {
  teardown();
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-empty-catch': 'error'

docs.title: no-eslint-disable
docs.text: Prevent use of eslint-disable comments; fix the underlying issue instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
eslint-disable comments suppress rule violations rather than fixing them. They accumulate over time, making it impossible to know whether a rule violation is intentional or forgotten. A zero-tolerance codebase should have no suppression comments: every violation should be fixed at its source.

Flagged comment forms:

  • // eslint-disable
  • // eslint-disable-next-line
  • // eslint-disable-line
  • /* eslint-disable */ block comments

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Fix the underlying issue instead of disabling the rule
const MAX_SIZE = 100;
if (items.length > MAX_SIZE) {
  throw new Error('Too many items');
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// eslint-disable-next-line zero-tolerance/no-magic-numbers
if (items.length > 100) {
  /* ... */
}

/* eslint-disable zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal */
throw 'something went wrong';
/* eslint-enable zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal */

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-eslint-disable': 'error'

docs.title: no-export-alias
docs.text: Prevent use of aliases in export statements; export values under their original names.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Export aliases (export { foo as bar }) create a disconnect between the internal name of a value and the name that consumers use. This makes it harder to search for a symbol across the codebase and increases the chance of confusion. Export the value directly, or rename it at the declaration site.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
export { fetchUser };
export { createOrder };
export { validateSchema };

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
export { fetchUser as getUser };
export { createOrder as placeOrder };
export { validateSchema as checkSchema };

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-export-alias': 'error'

docs.title: no-fetch-in-tests
docs.text: Disallow fetch usage in test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended off Strict off

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Direct HTTP calls in tests create network-dependent failures and blur the boundary between unit, integration, and end-to-end testing. Prefer injecting a client, using a mock transport, or keeping real HTTP behind explicit integration-test helpers.

This rule is opt-in and disabled by default in the built-in presets. It only runs in recognized test files: *.test.*, *.spec.*, *.e2e.*, *.integration.*, and files under __tests__/.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const client = createMockClient({
  '/users/1': { id: '1', name: 'Ada' },
});

await loadUser(client, '1');

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
await fetch('/users/1');
await globalThis.fetch('/users/1');
await window.fetch('/users/1');
