docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-fetch-in-tests': 'error'

docs.title: no-flag-argument
docs.text: Disallow boolean flag parameters in function signatures.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Boolean flag arguments usually indicate a function doing more than one thing. Prefer explicit methods, a command object, or a parameter object (Remove Flag Argument, Introduce Parameter Object, Replace Function with Command).

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function renderPreview(): string {
  return 'preview';
}

function renderFull(): string {
  return 'full';
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function render(isPreview: boolean): string {
  return isPreview ? 'preview' : 'full';
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-flag-argument': 'error'

docs.title: no-floating-promises
docs.text: Disallow unhandled promise expressions. Promise-producing expressions must be explicitly handled with await, void, .catch(...), or then(..., onRejected).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Floating promises frequently hide failures and create non-deterministic behavior. This rule enforces explicit handling so async intent is visible and rejected promises are not silently ignored.

The rule checks explicit promise-producing patterns such as:

  • dynamic import(...)
  • new Promise(...)
  • async IIFE calls ((async () => ...)())
  • promise chains using .then(...), .catch(...), and .finally(...)
  • Promise static calls such as Promise.resolve(...) and Promise.all(...)

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
await import('./worker');

void bootstrapAsync();

fetchData().catch(handleError);

fetchData().then(handleData, handleError);

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
import('./worker');

Promise.resolve(42);

(async () => {
  await bootstrapAsync();
})();

fetchData().then(handleData);

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-floating-promises': 'error'

docs.title: no-for-in
docs.text: Disallow for..in loops.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: for..in iterates enumerable keys across an object's prototype chain, which can introduce surprising behavior. Prefer Object.keys, Object.values, or Object.entries for predictable own-property iteration.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
for (const key of Object.keys(record)) {
  handle(key);
}

for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(record)) {
  handlePair(key, value);
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
for (const key in record) {
  handle(key);
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-for-in': 'error'

docs.title: Autofix Notes
docs.text:
Autofix rewrites:

for (const key in record) {
  handle(key);
}

to:

for (const key of Object.keys(record)) {
  handle(key);
}

docs.title: no-identical-branches
docs.text: Disallow identical branches in conditionals.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: If both branches of an if/else or ternary expression are identical, the condition is unnecessary noise. Consolidate shared logic (Consolidate Duplicate Conditional Fragments) and simplify the code.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
if (enabled) {
  runFastPath();
} else {
  runSafePath();
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (enabled) {
  execute();
} else {
  execute();
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-identical-branches': 'error'

docs.title: no-identical-expressions
docs.text: Disallow identical expressions on both sides of a binary or logical operator (Sonar S1764).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
When the left and right operands of a binary or logical expression are identical, the result is always deterministic — and almost certainly a copy-paste error. For example, a === a is always true, x || x is always x, and n - n is always 0.

Checked operators: ===, !==, ==, !=, &&, ||, ??, +, -, /, %

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
if (a === b) {
  /* ... */
}

const result = left || right;

const diff = endDate - startDate;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (value === value) {
  /* always true */
}

const x = obj || obj; // always obj

const zero = count - count; // always 0

if (user && user) {
  /* user is checked twice */
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-identical-expressions': 'error'

docs.title: no-inline-type-import
docs.text: Disallow TypeScript inline type imports using import("...").

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Inline type import queries make dependencies harder to scan and enforce inconsistently across files. Requiring top-level import type statements keeps imports explicit, grouped, and easier to maintain.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
import type { PipelineContext } from '../types/pipeline';
import type { Program, SourceFile, TypeChecker } from 'typescript';

interface ISourceLoadResult {
  readonly context: PipelineContext;
  readonly checker: TypeChecker;
  readonly options: Program;
  readonly sourceFiles: readonly SourceFile[];
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
interface ISourceLoadResult {
  readonly context: import('../types/pipeline').PipelineContext;
  readonly checker: import('typescript').TypeChecker;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-inline-type-import': 'error'

docs.title: no-jest-have-been-called
docs.text: Prohibit imprecise call-assertion matchers; use toHaveBeenCalledTimes and toHaveBeenNthCalledWith instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
toHaveBeenCalled / toBeCalled only asserts that a mock was called at least once without specifying how many times. toHaveBeenCalledWith / toBeCalledWith does not specify which invocation is being checked. toHaveBeenLastCalledWith / toLastCalledWith implicitly asserts only the final invocation, silently ignoring all others. All of these matchers can pass for the wrong reasons, producing false confidence.

Using toHaveBeenCalledTimes(n) forces you to declare the exact call count. Using toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(n, ...args) forces you to assert the arguments for a specific invocation. Together they produce precise, non-ambiguous test assertions.

Banned Replacement toHaveBeenCalled toHaveBeenCalledTimes toBeCalled toHaveBeenCalledTimes toHaveBeenCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith toBeCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith toHaveBeenLastCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith toLastCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);

expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(1, 'expected-argument');

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalled();
expect(mockFn).toBeCalled();

expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalledWith('expected-argument');
expect(mockFn).toBeCalledWith('expected-argument');

expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenLastCalledWith('expected-argument');
expect(mockFn).toLastCalledWith('expected-argument');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-jest-have-been-called': 'error'

docs.title: no-labels
docs.text: Disallow labeled statements.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Labels introduce goto-like control flow (break label, continue label) that makes code harder to follow and maintain. Prefer extracting logic into functions or using clearer loop structures.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
for (const row of rows) {
  if (!row.valid) {
    break;
  }
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
outer: for (const row of rows) {
  for (const cell of row.cells) {
    if (!cell.valid) {
      break outer;
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-labels': 'error'

docs.title: no-literal-property-unions
docs.text: Require property literal unions to use named domain types.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Property contracts that use literal unions hide finite or constrained domains inside object shapes. Defining the domain as a named type gives the allowed values a reusable name and keeps interface, type literal, and class property declarations from repeating raw values.

For string and number domains, prefer an enum. Bigint literal unions are allowed because TypeScript enums cannot represent bigint values, and there is no universally accepted lightweight pattern to replace them.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
enum SearchMode {
  TreeSitter = 'tree-sitter',
  RipgrepPrefilter = 'ripgrep-prefilter',
  TextHint = 'text-hint',
}

export interface IAstLangSearchMatch {
  path: string;
  line: number;
  column: number;
  text: string;
  matchedTerm: string;
  mode: SearchMode;
}

enum ExitCode {
  Success = 0,
  Failure = 1,
}

interface IProcessResult {
  exitCode: ExitCode;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
export interface IAstLangSearchMatch {
  path: string;
  line: number;
  column: number;
  text: string;
  matchedTerm: string;
  mode: 'tree-sitter' | 'ripgrep-prefilter' | 'text-hint';
}

interface IProcessResult {
  exitCode: 0 | 1;
}

type SearchMatch = {
  mode: 'tree-sitter' | 'text-hint' | undefined;
};

interface ISearchMatch {
  slug: `a-${string}` | `b-${string}`;
}

interface ISearchMatch {
  mode: 'tree-sitter' | string;
}

interface ISearchMatch {
  value: true | 'yes';
}

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This rule checks interface property signatures, type literal property signatures, class properties, and abstract class properties. It ignores function parameters and type aliases because those are covered by no-literal-unions.

Pure boolean property unions (true | false) are allowed because they represent the full boolean domain and do not benefit from a named domain. Bigint literal unions are also allowed because TypeScript enums cannot represent bigint values. Literal unions mixed with non-boolean members, widened types, or nullish members are still reported.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-literal-property-unions': 'error'

docs.title: no-literal-unions
docs.text: Ban literal union types in favour of enums.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (limited) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Literal union types ("active" | "inactive") scatter magic values throughout the codebase, are not refactor-safe, and provide no single place to enumerate valid values. The same problem exists when a type alias hides those values behind typeof references to same-file literal-valued const declarations. TypeScript enums give string and number domains a canonical home, making them easy to discover, iterate over, and refactor.

Property declarations are handled by no-literal-property-unions, which reports on the property name and keeps property value domains separate from general type aliases and parameters.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
enum Status {
  Active = 'active',
  Inactive = 'inactive',
}

enum Direction {
  North,
  South,
  East,
  West,
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
type Status = 'active' | 'inactive';

type Direction = 'north' | 'south' | 'east' | 'west';

type Size = 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg' | 'xl';

const ACTIVE = 'active';
const INACTIVE = 'inactive';

type Status = typeof ACTIVE | typeof INACTIVE;

const DEFAULT_STATUS = `default`;

type StatusName = typeof DEFAULT_STATUS | 'custom';

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
Pure boolean unions (true | false) are exempt because they represent the full boolean domain and do not benefit from an enum:

// ✅ Allowed — exhaustive boolean union
type Toggle = true | false;

The same exemption applies when a type alias references same-file boolean literal const declarations:

const ENABLED = true;
const DISABLED = false;

type Toggle = typeof ENABLED | typeof DISABLED;

Literal unions inside built-in TypeScript utility types are also exempt because they commonly describe property keys, filters, or type transformations rather than enum domains:

// Allowed: property-key selection
type PublicUser = Omit<User, 'password' | 'token'>;
type NamedUser = Pick<User, 'id' | 'name'>;
type UsersByRole = Record<'admin' | 'guest', User>;

// Allowed: utility filtering and transformation
type NonDraftStatus = Exclude<Status, 'draft' | 'archived'>;
type VisibleStatus = Extract<Status, 'published' | 'scheduled'>;
type RequiredStatus = NonNullable<'active' | 'inactive' | null>;

The utility-type exemption is limited to known TypeScript utility types. Custom generics are still checked:

// Still reported
type WrappedStatus = CustomUtility<'active' | 'inactive'>;

docs.title: Autofix Behavior
docs.text: This rule can auto-convert string-only type aliases to enums. That includes direct string literal unions and type aliases that reference same-file string literal const declarations through typeof.

docs.title: Auto-fixable
docs.text:
type Status = 'active' | 'inactive';
// becomes:
enum Status {
  Active = 'active',
  Inactive = 'inactive',
}

const ACTIVE = 'active';
const INACTIVE = 'inactive';

type Status = typeof ACTIVE | typeof INACTIVE;
// becomes:
enum Status {
  ACTIVE = 'active',
  INACTIVE = 'inactive',
}

const ACTIVE = 'active';

type Status = typeof ACTIVE | 'inactive';
// becomes:
enum Status {
  ACTIVE = 'active',
  Inactive = 'inactive',
}

docs.title: Not auto-fixable
docs.text:
• Generic aliases (for example type Status<T> = 'active' | 'inactive')
  • Non-alias unions (for example function parameter unions)
  • Property declarations (handled by no-literal-property-unions)
  • Unions that contain number members, boolean members, or unresolved typeof members

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-literal-unions': 'error'

docs.title: no-magic-numbers
docs.text: Disallow raw numeric literals in expressions; use named constants instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Magic numbers are numeric literals that appear without explanation. They make code hard to read (what does 86400 mean?) and hard to maintain (every occurrence must be updated if the value changes). Extracting them into named constants adds meaning and creates a single source of truth.

Allowed values: 0, 1, and -1 are universally understood and are always permitted.

Allowed locations:

  • const variable initialisers — this is how you define a named constant
  • TypeScript enum member values

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const SECONDS_PER_DAY = 86400;
const MAX_RETRIES = 3;
const HTTP_OK = 200;

// Allowed sentinel values
const index = -1;
const first = arr[0];
const length = arr.length + 1;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
setTimeout(callback, 86400 * 1000);

if (response.status === 200) {
  /* ... */
}

let retries = 3; // let or var declaration — not a named const

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-magic-numbers': 'error'

docs.title: no-magic-strings
docs.text: Disallow magic string literals in comparisons and switch-case clauses; use named constants instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
String literals used in comparisons (=== 'admin') or switch cases are magic strings: their meaning is implicit, they can be mis-typed silently, and every occurrence must be updated if the value changes. Defining them as named constants (const ROLE_ADMIN = 'admin') adds meaning and creates a single source of truth.

Checked locations:

  • ===, !==, ==, != binary comparisons
  • switch case test values

typeof comparisons are exempt (for example, typeof value === 'string').

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const ROLE_ADMIN = 'admin';
const STATUS_ACTIVE = 'active';

if (user.role === ROLE_ADMIN) {
  /* ... */
}

switch (user.status) {
  case STATUS_ACTIVE:
    break;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (user.role === 'admin') {
  /* ... */
}

switch (user.status) {
  case 'active':
    break;
  case 'inactive':
    break;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule accepts an optional options object:

'zero-tolerance/no-magic-strings': [
  'error',
  {
    checkComparisons: true,
    checkSwitchCases: true,
    ignoreValues: ['production'],
  },
]

docs.title: no-mock-implementation
docs.text: Prohibit persistent mock setup methods; use their Once variants to prevent test bleeds.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
mockImplementation, mockReturnValue, mockResolvedValue, and mockRejectedValue configure a mock persistently for the lifetime of the test suite (or until manually reset). This means a mock configured in one test silently bleeds into subsequent tests, leading to hard-to-diagnose ordering-dependent failures.

The Once variants (mockImplementationOnce, mockReturnValueOnce, mockResolvedValueOnce, mockRejectedValueOnce) only apply for a single call and then revert. This eliminates the source of test bleeds entirely.

Banned Replacement mockImplementation mockImplementationOnce mockReturnValue mockReturnValueOnce mockResolvedValue mockResolvedValueOnce mockRejectedValue mockRejectedValueOnce

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
jest.fn().mockImplementationOnce(() => 'value');
jest.fn().mockReturnValueOnce(42);
jest.fn().mockResolvedValueOnce({ data: 'ok' });
jest.fn().mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error('failed'));

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
jest.fn().mockImplementation(() => 'value');
jest.fn().mockReturnValue(42);
jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ data: 'ok' });
jest.fn().mockRejectedValue(new Error('failed'));

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-mock-implementation': 'error'

docs.title: no-non-null-assertion
docs.text: Disallow non-null assertions using the ! postfix operator.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: The ! postfix operator tells TypeScript to treat an expression as non-null without any runtime check. If the value is actually null or undefined at runtime, the assertion causes a crash rather than a handled error. Use optional chaining (?.), nullish coalescing (??), or an explicit null check instead.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Optional chaining
const name = user?.profile?.name;

// Nullish coalescing
const displayName = user?.name ?? 'Anonymous';

// Explicit null check
if (element !== null) {
  element.focus();
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const name = user!.profile!.name;

document.getElementById('root')!.innerHTML = '';

const value = maybeNull!;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-non-null-assertion': 'error'

docs.title: no-object-mutation
docs.text: Disallow direct object-property mutation.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Mutating object properties in place introduces side effects and makes state transitions harder to trace. Immutable updates improve predictability.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const next = { ...state, count: state.count + 1 };

class AppError extends Error {
  constructor(message: string, context?: Record<string, unknown>) {
    super(message);
    this.name = this.constructor.name;
    this.context = context;
  }
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text: state.count = state.count + 1;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/no-object-mutation': 'error'

docs.title: no-parameter-reassign
docs.text: Disallow assignments and updates to function parameters.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Reassigning parameters is a common code smell that makes control flow harder to follow and hides intent. Prefer introducing a new local variable (Extract Variable / Split Variable) instead of mutating the input parameter.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function normalize(value: string): string {
  let normalized = value.trim();
  normalized = normalized.toLowerCase();
  return normalized;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function normalize(value: string): string {
  value = value.trim();
  value = value.toLowerCase();
  return value;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-parameter-reassign': 'error'

docs.title: no-parent-internal-access
docs.text: Disallow parent-relative access into protected internal directories such as src.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended off Strict off

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Parent-relative paths like ../src/... and ../../src/... usually mean one module is reaching into another module's internal implementation instead of consuming its public entrypoint. That creates tight coupling, makes refactors harder, and breaks the expectation that protected directories such as src, app, or internal are self-contained boundaries.

This rule is opt-in and disabled by default in the built-in presets.

By default, the rule protects the src directory name. You can override that list with the protectedDirectories option.

Matching is intentionally narrow:

  • only parent-relative paths are checked
  • only the first concrete directory reached after .. traversal is matched
  • protectedDirectories entries are trimmed, deduplicated, and empty entries are ignored

This rule checks:

  • import ... from '...'
  • import('...')
  • require('...')
  • TypeScript import x = require('...')
  • direct re-exports such as export { x } from '...' and export * from '...'

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
import { feature } from '../shared/feature';
import localFeature from './src/local-feature';
export { childFeature } from './src/child-feature';
import { nestedFeature } from '../shared/src/feature';

import { parentValue } from '../parent';
const localValue = parentValue;
export { localValue };

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
import { feature } from '../src/feature';
const legacyFeature = require('../../src/legacy-feature');
const dynamicFeature = await import('../src/dynamic-feature');
import feature = require('../src/feature');

export { sharedFeature } from '../src/shared-feature';
export * from '../../src/shared-feature';

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Default configuration:

'zero-tolerance/no-parent-internal-access': 'error'

Custom protected directory names:

'zero-tolerance/no-parent-internal-access': [
  'error',
  {
    protectedDirectories: ['src', 'app', 'internal'],
  },
]

This matches ../src/foo and ../../app/foo, but not ../shared/src/foo because shared is the first traversed directory.

docs.title: no-query-side-effects
docs.text: Disallow side effects in query-style functions (get*, is*, has*, can*, should*).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Query functions should answer questions without mutating state. Mixing reads and writes increases coupling and makes behavior harder to reason about. This rule enforces Separate Query from Modifier.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function getTotal(items: number[]): number {
  return items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item, 0);
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function getTotal(items: number[]): number {
  items.push(0);
  return items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item, 0);
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-query-side-effects': 'error'

docs.title: no-re-export
docs.text: Disallow direct and pass-through re-export statements from parent or ancestor modules in non-barrel files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Re-export statements that reach outside the current directory (../) create upward dependencies that violate proper module hierarchy. That includes direct re-exports such as export { foo } from '../foo' and pass-through patterns such as import { foo } from '../foo'; export { foo };. Statement ordering does not matter: export { foo }; import { foo } from '../foo'; is also reported. Only barrel files (index.*) are exempt, as their sole purpose is to aggregate exports. All other files must not re-export from peer modules (../sibling) or ancestors (../../parent, ../../../grandparent).

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The barrel-file exemption applies only to single-extension index files such as index.ts, index.js, and index.mts.
  • Double-extension files such as index.d.ts, index.test.ts, and index.spec.js are not treated as barrel files and are still checked by this rule.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Direct exports
export { foo, bar };
export const value = 42;
export function myFunction() {}

// Re-exports from children (always allowed)
export { childFunction } from './child';
export * from './child/utils';

// Exporting a local binding remains allowed
import { parentValue } from '../parent';
const localValue = parentValue;
export { localValue };

// In a barrel file (index.ts), upward re-exports are allowed
// export { peerFunction } from '../sibling';
// export * from '../../parent';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// Re-exports from peers are not allowed in non-barrel files
export { peerFunction } from '../sibling';
export * from '../utils';

// Re-exports from parents are not allowed
export { parentFunction } from '../../parent';
export * from '../../grandparent';

// Pass-through re-exports from parents are also not allowed
import { peerFunction } from '../sibling';
export { peerFunction };

import parentDefault from '../../parent';
export default parentDefault;

// Re-exports from grandparents are not allowed
export { grandparentFunction } from '../../../ancestor';
export * as ns from '../../../../distant-ancestor';

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-re-export': 'error'

docs.title: no-redundant-boolean
docs.text: Disallow redundant comparisons to true or false (Sonar S1125).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Comparing a boolean expression to true or false adds noise without adding meaning. isValid === true is identical to isValid; isActive !== false is identical to isActive. These redundant comparisons obscure intent and are commonly introduced by developers who are uncertain about the type of the value.

Checked operators: ===, !==

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
if (isValid) {
  /* ... */
}

if (!isActive) {
  /* ... */
}

return hasPermission;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (isValid === true) {
  /* ... */
}

if (isActive !== false) {
  /* ... */
}

return hasPermission === true;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-redundant-boolean': 'error'

docs.title: no-restricted-imports-in-tests
docs.text: Disallow configured dependency imports in test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended off Strict off

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Tests should make expensive boundaries explicit. Directly importing filesystem, process, and network modules inside test files often leads to tests that depend on local machine state, real sockets, subprocesses, or external services. This rule is opt-in so projects can adopt it where those boundaries matter most.

By default, the rule restricts these module roots in test files:

axios, child_process, dgram, dns, fs, got, http, http2, https, net, node-fetch, tls, undici, worker_threads

The node: protocol is normalized before matching, so node:fs, node:fs/promises, and fs/promises all match the fs root. Entries ending in /* are also normalized, so configured values such as fs/* and node:fs/* protect the same root.

This rule checks:

  • import ... from '...'
  • import('...')
  • require('...')
  • TypeScript import x = require('...')
  • direct re-exports such as export { x } from '...' and export * from '...'

It only runs in recognized test files: *.test.*, *.spec.*, *.e2e.*, *.integration.*, and files under __tests__/.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
import { createFixture } from './fixtures';
import { render } from '@testing-library/react';

const client = createMockClient();
const result = await runScenario(client);

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
import fs from 'fs';
import { readFile } from 'node:fs/promises';
import { execFile } from 'node:child_process';
import axios from 'axios';

const http = require('http');
const net = await import('node:net');
import workerThreads = require('node:worker_threads');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Default configuration:

'zero-tolerance/no-restricted-imports-in-tests': 'error'

Custom restricted module roots:

'zero-tolerance/no-restricted-imports-in-tests': [
  'error',
  {
    modules: ['fs', 'node:fs/*', 'child_process', 'axios', '@company/database'],
  },
]

Configured module names are trimmed, lowercased, normalized by removing node:, and matched by module root. For example, fs matches fs, fs/promises, node:fs, and node:fs/promises.

docs.title: no-set-interval-in-tests
docs.text: Disallow setInterval usage in test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Intervals in tests create background work that can leak across assertions, suites, or worker lifetimes. Prefer fake timers, direct event triggering, or a test helper that exposes a deterministic signal.

This rule only runs in recognized test files: *.test.*, *.spec.*, *.e2e.*, *.integration.*, and files under __tests__/.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
vi.useFakeTimers();

const subscription = startPolling();
vi.advanceTimersByTime(1000);
subscription.stop();

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
setInterval(poll, 1000);
global.setInterval(poll, 1000);
window.setInterval(poll, 1000);

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-set-interval-in-tests': 'error'

docs.title: no-set-timeout-in-tests
docs.text: Disallow setTimeout usage in test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Real-time sleeps make tests slower and timing-dependent. A passing test can still be waiting longer than necessary, while a failing test may only fail under CI load or on slower machines. Prefer fake timers, explicit async coordination, or framework polling helpers that wait for an observable condition.

This rule only runs in recognized test files: *.test.*, *.spec.*, *.e2e.*, *.integration.*, and files under __tests__/.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
vi.useFakeTimers();

const pending = promiseUnderTest();
vi.advanceTimersByTime(100);
await pending;

await waitFor(() => expect(screen.getByText('Done')).toBeVisible());

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
setTimeout(assertLater, 100);
globalThis.setTimeout(assertLater, 100);
window.setTimeout(assertLater, 100);

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-set-timeout-in-tests': 'error'

docs.title: no-test-interface-declaration
docs.text: Disallow interface declarations in test files; import production types instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Declaring an interface inside a test file is almost always a sign that the production code is not exporting a proper type contract. When a test author creates a local interface to type mock objects or fixtures, that type drifts independently from the real production shape. If the production type changes, the test's copy won't break, silently hiding regressions.

The correct fix is to define the interface in the production module and import it in the test.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// user.ts (production)
export interface IUser {
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

// user.test.ts
import type { IUser } from './user';

const mockUser: IUser = { name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@example.com' };

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// user.test.ts
interface IUser {
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

const mockUser: IUser = { name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@example.com' };

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-test-interface-declaration': 'error'

docs.title: no-throw-literal
docs.text: Disallow throwing literals, plain objects, or template strings; always throw a new Error() instance.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Only Error instances (and subclasses) include a stack trace. Throwing a string, object, number, or template literal discards diagnostic information and can break error-handling code that expects an Error.

By default, this rule only allows:

  • throw new ... expressions (for example throw new Error(...))
  • re-throwing the active catch parameter (for example catch (err) { throw err; })

You can opt into allowing throw <call>, throw <member>, and throw await <expr> with rule options.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
throw new Error('something went wrong');

throw new TypeError('expected a string');

throw new RangeError('value out of range');

class ValidationError extends Error {}
throw new ValidationError('invalid input');

// Re-throwing a caught error
try {
  riskyOp();
} catch (err) {
  throw err;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
throw 'something went wrong';

throw 404;

throw { message: 'error', code: 500 };

throw `failed: ${reason}`;

throw condition ? 'error-a' : 'error-b';

throw createError('message');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule accepts an optional options object:

'zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal': [
  'error',
  {
    allowThrowingCallExpressions: false,
    allowThrowingMemberExpressions: false,
    allowThrowingAwaitExpressions: false,
  },
]

docs.title: no-type-assertion
docs.text: Prevent use of TypeScript type assertions (as and angle-bracket assertions) outside of test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Type assertions tell the TypeScript compiler to trust you over the type system. They are a common source of runtime errors because they bypass inference and structural checks. In production code, explicit type guards, generic functions, or proper data modelling should be used instead.

The rule allows as unknown and <unknown> in test files (.test.*, .spec.*, .e2e.*, .integration.*, and files under __tests__/) because this pattern is common in test utilities and rule tester setups.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Use a type guard instead
function isUser(value: unknown): value is IUser {
  return typeof value === 'object' && value !== null && 'name' in value && 'email' in value;
}

// Generic function with a constraint
function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined {
  return arr[0];
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const user = response.data as IUser;

const element = document.querySelector('#app') as HTMLDivElement;

const value = <MyType>getValue();

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-type-assertion': 'error'

docs.title: no-with
docs.text: Disallow with statements.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: with changes scope resolution rules in ways that are hard to reason about and optimize. It is disallowed in strict mode and should be replaced with explicit object property access.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: const fullName = person.firstName + person.lastName;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
with (person) {
  fullName = firstName + lastName;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-with': 'error'

docs.title: prefer-guard-clauses
docs.text: Prefer guard clauses over else blocks after terminating if branches.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: When an if branch already returns or throws, keeping an else block adds unnecessary nesting. Flattening with guard clauses improves readability and aligns with Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
if (!user) {
  return;
}
processUser(user);

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (!user) {
  return;
} else {
  processUser(user);
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/prefer-guard-clauses': 'error'

docs.title: prefer-nullish-coalescing
docs.text: Prefer using the nullish coalescing operator (??) instead of ternary expressions that only guard against null/undefined.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: A nullish guard ternary (value != null ? value : fallback) is functionally equivalent to value ?? fallback, but the coalescing operator is shorter and more expressive about the intent to fall back only for null/undefined values.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const userName = input ?? 'guest';
const description = config?.description ?? 'Not provided';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const userName = input != null ? input : 'guest';
const description = config == null ? 'Not provided' : config.description;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/prefer-nullish-coalescing': 'error'

docs.title: prefer-readonly-parameters
docs.text: Prefer readonly typing for object and array-like function parameters.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Function parameters are inputs. Treating object and array parameters as readonly prevents accidental mutation and preserves referential transparency.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function format(user: Readonly<User>): string {
  return user.name;
}

class Service {
  constructor(private items: readonly string[]) {}
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function format(user: User): string {
  return user.name;
}

class Service {
  constructor(private items: string[]) {}
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/prefer-readonly-parameters': 'error'

docs.title: Autofix Notes
docs.text:
Autofix can safely:

  • wrap mutable type references as Readonly<T>
  • rewrite mutable array and tuple annotations to readonly ...
  • apply those rewrites to constructor parameter properties as well

Autofix intentionally skips structural rewrites such as mutable inline object type literals.

docs.title: prefer-result-return
docs.text: Prefer returning Result-style values instead of throwing.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended off Strict warn

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Returning typed error values keeps failures explicit in function signatures and avoids hidden control-flow jumps from thrown exceptions.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function parse(input: string) {
  return { ok: false as const, error: 'invalid' };
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function parse(input: string) {
  throw new Error('invalid');
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/prefer-result-return': 'warn'

docs.title: prefer-shortcut-return
docs.text: Prefer shortcut boolean returns over if branches that return true/false.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: When code branches only to return opposite boolean literals, the extra control flow is unnecessary. A direct return is shorter and easier to read.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function isReady(value: unknown): boolean {
  return !!value;
}

function isDisabled(value: unknown): boolean {
  return !value;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function isReady(value: unknown): boolean {
  if (value) return true;
  return false;
}

function isDisabled(value: unknown): boolean {
  if (value) {
    return false;
  } else {
    return true;
  }
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/prefer-shortcut-return': 'error'

docs.title: prefer-string-raw
docs.text:
Prefer String.raw for string literals that contain escaped backslashes.

This mirrors Sonar rule typescript:S7780.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Strings containing escaped backslashes are harder to read and easier to get wrong. Using String.raw avoids double escaping and keeps path-like and regex-source text easier to maintain.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const path = String.raw`C:\Users\dev\repo`;
const regexSource = String.raw`\d+\w+`;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const path = 'C:\\Users\\dev\\repo';
const regexSource = '\\d+\\w+';

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/prefer-string-raw': 'error'

docs.title: Autofix Notes
docs.text: Autofix rewrites eligible string literals to String.raw\`form. It intentionally skips unsafe cases such as literals containing template interpolation markers (for example,${...}`) or backticks.

docs.title: require-bdd-spec
docs.text: Enforce that every TypeScript source file has a valid sibling .ts.bdd.json BDD specification file.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended off Strict off

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Every non-test TypeScript source file in the plugin must be accompanied by a sibling .ts.bdd.json file that documents its behaviour in BDD (Given/When/Then) format. This rule validates that the file exists and that its content is semantically valid: required fields are present, types are correct, the specifications array is non-empty, all scenario names start with "should", and the module.exports list exactly matches the named exports in the source file.

Violations are reported as an aggregated list of errors so all problems are visible in a single ESLint run.

This rule is intentionally opt-in and is disabled by default in the built-in recommended and strict presets. Enable it explicitly for repositories that require BDD spec files.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
A source file rules/my-rule.ts with a sibling rules/my-rule.ts.bdd.json:

{
  "$schema": "../../../../bdd-spec.schema.json",
  "schemaVersion": "1.0.0",
  "sourceFile": "packages/plugin/src/rules/my-rule.ts",
  "module": {
    "name": "my-rule",
    "description": "Enforces a specific coding standard",
    "exports": ["myRule"]
  },
  "specifications": [
    {
      "feature": "My rule feature",
      "scenarios": [
        {
          "name": "should report a violation when the pattern is found",
          "given": "a TypeScript source file containing the prohibited pattern",
          "when": "ESLint runs the rule",
          "then": "a violation is reported with the correct message"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
Missing sibling .ts.bdd.json:

// my-rule.ts exists but my-rule.ts.bdd.json is absent

BDD spec with a scenario name that does not start with "should":

{
  "scenarios": [
    {
      "name": "validates the input",
      "given": "...",
      "when": "...",
      "then": "..."
    }
  ]
}

BDD spec whose module.exports does not match the source file's named exports:

{
  "module": {
    "exports": ["myRule", "nonExistentExport"]
  }
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-bdd-spec': 'error'

docs.title: require-clean-barrel
docs.text: Require barrel files (index.*) to contain only module re-export declarations.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Barrel files should stay focused on aggregation. Allowing imports, declarations, executable statements, or local exports inside index.* files mixes concerns and makes module boundaries harder to reason about.

When an index.* file contains at least one module re-export declaration, this rule treats it as a barrel and enforces module re-exports only, such as:

  • export { foo } from './foo'
  • export * from './foo'
  • export type { Foo } from './types'

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
export { createClient } from './create-client';
export { parseConfig } from './parse-config';
export type { ClientOptions } from './types';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
import { createClient } from './create-client';
export { createClient } from './create-client';

export const version = '1.0.0';

const helper = () => {};
export { helper };

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-clean-barrel': 'error'

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The rule applies only to single-extension barrel files such as index.ts, index.js, and index.mts.
  • Double-extension files such as index.d.ts, index.test.ts, and index.spec.js are ignored.

docs.title: require-exported-object-type
docs.text: Require exported object constants to declare an explicit type annotation.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Exported object constants become part of a module's public contract. Requiring an explicit annotation prevents large inferred structural types from leaking through exports and keeps the intended shape readable even when the value is wrapped in Object.freeze(...).

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
export const StatusMap: Readonly<Record<string, string>> = Object.freeze({
  Active: 'active',
  Inactive: 'inactive',
});

type StatusLookup = Record<string, string>;

const StatusMap: StatusLookup = {
  Active: 'active',
};

export { StatusMap };

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
export const StatusMap = Object.freeze({
  Active: 'active',
  Inactive: 'inactive',
});

const StatusMap = {
  Active: 'active',
};

export { StatusMap };

The rule only applies when the exported const initializer is an object literal or Object.freeze({ ... }). Calls such as Object.freeze() or Object.freeze(...values) are ignored because they do not expose a direct frozen object literal shape.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-exported-object-type': 'error'

docs.title: require-interface-prefix
docs.text: Enforce that TypeScript interface names start with I (capital letter) followed by an uppercase character.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Prefixing interface names with I makes it immediately clear in code that a type is an interface rather than a class or type alias. This convention improves readability, especially in large codebases where interfaces are heavily used.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
interface IUser {
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

interface IRepository<T> {
  findById(id: string): Promise<T>;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
interface User {
  name: string;
}

interface userProfile {
  age: number;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options. Enable it in your ESLint config:

'zero-tolerance/require-interface-prefix': 'error'

docs.title: require-jsdoc-anonymous-functions
docs.text: Require JSDoc documentation comments on anonymous function-like constructs outside of test files, such as anonymous default exports, computed methods without identifier keys, and inline anonymous callbacks. Anonymous callbacks passed to well-known test APIs such as describe, it, test, beforeEach, and afterAll are excluded.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended off Strict warn

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Anonymous functions hide intent at the callsite and can make behavior harder to understand in larger codebases. Requiring JSDoc on anonymous function-like constructs makes their purpose explicit and improves generated documentation quality.

This rule is automatically skipped in test files (.test.* / .spec.*). It also skips anonymous callbacks passed to common test framework APIs and hook helpers, including chained forms such as describe.only(...), test.skip.each(...), and test.beforeEach(...).

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Creates the default app configuration.
 */
export default function () {
  return { retries: 3 };
}

class Registry {
  /**
   * Handles computed command dispatch.
   */
  ['run']() {}
}

describe('CLI knowledge-base command', () => {
  it('should register the command', () => {
    expect(getKnowledgeBaseCommand()).toBeDefined();
  });
});

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
export default function () {
  return { retries: 3 };
}

class Registry {
  ['run']() {}
}

registerCommand(() => {
  return buildCommand();
});

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-jsdoc-anonymous-functions': 'warn'

docs.title: Autofix Notes
docs.text:
Autofix can insert a missing JSDoc block for standalone anonymous targets such as default export functions.

Autofix intentionally skips unsafe insertion targets such as inline expressions and multi-declarator variable statements.

docs.title: Test files and test callbacks are exempt
docs.text:
Functions inside files matching *.test.ts, *.spec.ts, *.test.js, *.spec.js (and their JSX equivalents) are not checked.

Anonymous callbacks passed to well-known test APIs such as describe, it, test, beforeAll, beforeEach, afterAll, and afterEach are also not checked, even when they appear in non-test files.

docs.title: require-jsdoc-functions
docs.text: Require JSDoc documentation comments on named function-like constructs outside of test files, including declarations, named variable-assigned function expressions, named arrow functions, class methods, static methods, class field functions, and object methods.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
JSDoc comments are essential for self-documenting code and support IDE tooling, generated API documentation, and clear intent communication to future maintainers. This rule is automatically skipped in test files (.test.* / .spec.*).

Anonymous function-like constructs are enforced by the companion rule require-jsdoc-anonymous-functions.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Fetches a user record by ID.
 */
async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<IUser> {
  return db.users.findById(id);
}

/**
 * Validates that the given email address is well-formed.
 */
const isValidEmail = (email: string): boolean => /^[^@]+@[^@]+$/.test(email);

/**
 * Returns a normalized display label.
 */
export const getLabel = (input: string): string => input.trim();

class DedupeFactory {
  /**
   * Creates the dedupe service instance.
   */
  static createDedupeService(): DedupeService {
    return new DedupeService();
  }
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<IUser> {
  return db.users.findById(id);
}

const isValidEmail = (email: string): boolean => /^[^@]+@[^@]+$/.test(email);

class DedupeFactory {
  static createDedupeService(): DedupeService {
    return new DedupeService();
  }
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-jsdoc-functions': 'error'

docs.title: Autofix Notes
docs.text:
Autofix can:

  • insert a missing JSDoc block for standalone function declarations/expressions and members
  • append missing @param, @returns, and @throws tags to existing JSDoc blocks

Autofix intentionally skips unsafe insertion targets such as inline declarations and multi-declarator variable statements.

docs.title: Test files are exempt
docs.text: Functions inside files matching *.test.ts, *.spec.ts, *.test.js, *.spec.js (and their JSX equivalents) are not checked.

docs.title: require-node-protocol
docs.text: Require Node.js built-in module imports to use the node: protocol prefix.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Starting with Node.js 16, built-in modules can be imported using the node: protocol prefix (e.g., node:fs instead of fs). Using the protocol prefix makes it immediately clear that the import refers to a Node.js built-in module rather than a user-land package with the same name, improving readability and preventing potential name collisions.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
import fs from 'node:fs';
import path from 'node:path';
import { readFile } from 'node:fs/promises';
import os from 'node:os';
import { createServer } from 'node:http';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
import fs from 'fs';
import path from 'path';
import { readFile } from 'fs/promises';
import os from 'os';
import { createServer } from 'http';

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-node-protocol': 'error'

docs.title: require-optional-chaining
docs.text: Require optional chaining instead of repeated logical guard access.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Patterns like obj && obj.value or fn && fn() duplicate the same guard expression and make code harder to read. Optional chaining expresses the same intent directly in one expression and removes repetition.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const profile = user?.profile;
const result = fn?.();
const name = account?.owner?.name;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const profile = user && user.profile;
const result = fn && fn();
const name = account.owner && account.owner.name;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-optional-chaining': 'error'

docs.title: require-readonly-props
docs.text: Require JSX component props to be typed as readonly.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Component props are inputs and should be treated as immutable. Requiring readonly props makes mutation attempts fail at compile time and keeps component behavior predictable.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
type Props = { name: string };

function Greeting(props: Readonly<Props>) {
  return <div>{props.name}</div>;
}

function Greeting(props: { readonly name: string }) {
  return <div>{props.name}</div>;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
type Props = { name: string };

function Greeting(props: Props) {
  return <div>{props.name}</div>;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-readonly-props': 'error'

docs.title: require-test-description-style
docs.text: Enforce that it() and test() descriptions start with the word should.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Test descriptions starting with should form a consistent, human-readable specification language. Reading them aloud reveals the intended behaviour: "it should render correctly", "it should throw when the input is invalid". This convention makes test reports self-documenting and helps reviewers scan failures quickly.

The rule applies to it(), test(), it.only(), and test.only(). By default it skips it.skip() and test.skip() to avoid friction when temporarily disabling tests.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
it('should render correctly', () => {});

test('should throw when input is invalid', () => {});

it.only('should handle concurrent requests', () => {});

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
it('renders correctly', () => {});

test('handles errors', () => {});

it('Check that user is saved', () => {});

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule accepts an optional options object:

'zero-tolerance/require-test-description-style': [
  'error',
  {
    prefix: 'should',
    ignoreSkip: true,
  },
]

docs.title: Autofix Notes
docs.text: Autofix updates string-literal test descriptions by prepending the configured prefix (default should) and normalizing leading whitespace.

docs.title: require-union-type-alias
docs.text: Require inline union types with multiple type references to be extracted into named type aliases.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Inline union types that combine multiple named type references hide complex domain relationships inside property annotations, function parameters, and return types. Extracting these unions into named type aliases provides:

  • Discoverability: A named type alias makes the union searchable and identifiable across the codebase.
  • Reusability: The same union used in multiple locations can reference a single type alias, eliminating duplication.
  • Readability: Short, descriptive alias names are easier to read than long inline unions.

This rule complements no-literal-unions (which handles literal value unions like "active" | "inactive") and no-literal-property-unions (which handles literal property unions). It covers the remaining gap: inline unions of named type references.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
type ImportSource = DirectoryImportSourceSelection | SourceType | undefined;

interface IConfig {
  sourceType: Readonly<ImportSource>;
}

type Handler = RequestHandler | ResponseHandler;

function process(handler: Handler): void {
  // ...
}

// A single type reference with null/undefined is allowed
interface IResult {
  value: MyType | undefined;
}

// Keyword-only unions are allowed
interface IConfig {
  value: string | number;
}

// Type alias declarations are the goal state — never flagged
type SourceKind = DirectoryImportSourceSelection | SourceType;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
interface IConfig {
  sourceType: DirectoryImportSourceSelection | SourceType | undefined;
}

function process(input: TypeA | TypeB): void {
  // ...
}

interface IConfig {
  value: Readonly<TypeA | TypeB>;
}

class Service {
  handler: RequestHandler | ResponseHandler = null!;
}

const result: SuccessResult | ErrorResult = getResult();

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This rule checks every TSUnionType AST node regardless of position — interface properties, type literal properties, class properties, function parameters, return types, variable declarations, and generic type arguments.

A union type is allowed when:

  • It is the direct right-hand side of a type alias declaration (type X = A | B).
  • It contains fewer than two type reference members. Keyword types (string, number, boolean, undefined, null, void, never, unknown, any), literal types ("foo", 42, true), and other non-reference type nodes do not count toward the threshold.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-union-type-alias': 'error'

docs.title: sort-functions
docs.text: Require top-level function declarations to be sorted alphabetically by name.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (--fix) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Alphabetically ordered top-level functions make a module's API easy to navigate and reduce the chance of duplicate function definitions. The check is case-insensitive and applies to both function declarations and const variable declarations that initialise arrow functions or function expressions at the top level of the program. Methods, nested functions, and non-const variable declarations are not checked.

Autofix reorders whole declaration blocks when it is safe to do so. It preserves and moves own-line block comments that immediately precede a declaration, as well as same-line trailing comments. Line comments and other ambiguous interstitial comments between declarations are not moved and may cause the rule to report without applying --fix to avoid mangling results.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function buildReport() {
  /* ... */
}
function fetchData() {
  /* ... */
}
function validateInput() {
  /* ... */
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function validateInput() {
  /* ... */
}
function buildReport() {
  /* ... */
} // should come before validateInput
function fetchData() {
  /* ... */
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/sort-functions': 'error'

docs.title: sort-imports
docs.text: Require import declarations to be grouped and sorted: side-effect → builtin → external → parent → peer → index, with alphabetical ordering within each group.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Consistent import ordering reduces merge conflicts and makes it immediately clear where a dependency comes from. Imports must appear in six groups in order — side-effect (bare) imports, Node.js built-in modules (node: protocol), external packages, parent-directory (../) imports, peer (./) imports, and the index (.) import — with each group sorted alphabetically (case-insensitive).

Side-effect imports (import 'module') have no specifiers and often set up runtime prerequisites such as polyfills or reflection metadata. They must appear before all other imports.

Node.js built-in imports use the node: protocol prefix (e.g., node:path, node:fs) and are placed after side-effect imports but before third-party external packages.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// 1. Side-effect imports — alphabetical
import 'reflect-metadata';

// 2. Node.js built-in imports — alphabetical
import { readFile } from 'node:fs';
import { dirname } from 'node:path';

// 3. External packages — alphabetical
import express from 'express';
import { injectable } from 'inversify';

// 4. Parent imports — alphabetical
import { models } from '../models';
import { utils } from '../utils';

// 5. Peer imports — alphabetical
import { auth } from './auth';
import { users } from './users';

// 6. Index import
import self from '.';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// External import placed before side-effect import — wrong group order
import { injectable } from 'inversify';
import 'reflect-metadata';

// Peer import placed before external — wrong group order
import { auth } from './auth';
import express from 'express';

// Parent import placed before external — wrong group order
import { utils } from '../utils';
import lodash from 'lodash';

// External packages out of alphabetical order
import zlib from 'zlib';
import axios from 'axios'; // 'axios' should come before 'zlib'

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/sort-imports': 'error'
